This falls under the ux-usability category, system-status subcategory — Nielsen Heuristic 1. When users understand current system status, they can predict outcomes and determine next steps. Without feedback, users don't know if their action was received or how much effort remains. Critical in checkout flows, onboarding, file uploads, and form submissions. To detect: check for visible progress indicators in multi-step flows; check that interactive elements provide post-interaction state feedback; flag forms with no progress signal; check for post-submission success or failure states. Applies across both viewport and full page.

knowledge.rule: Labels, buttons, and icons must use vocabulary and visual conventions familiar to users, not internal or technical terminology. An interface that requires users to decode unfamiliar language or non-standard icons increases cognitive load and erodes trust.
knowledge.detail: Users arrive at an interface with vocabulary and mental models formed by their real-world experience and other products they use. When UI language reflects internal system architecture, developer terminology, or corporate jargon rather than the user's own vocabulary, comprehension requires extra effort. The same applies to icons — a widely-recognised icon (shopping cart, magnifying glass for search) maps to a user's real-world model; a novel or ambiguous icon forces guessing.
detection_signals: Check CTA and button labels for jargon or internal terminology — 'Initiate onboarding' instead of 'Get started', 'Submit query' instead of 'Search'
Check navigation labels: do they reflect user goals or system architecture? 'Module 3' vs 'Pricing', 'CMS' vs 'Manage content'
Check icon usage: non-standard or novel icons without text labels require users to guess meaning — flag unfamiliar icons used without accompanying text (see also kb-w-007)
Check error messages and system notifications for technical language — error codes, stack trace fragments, or developer-oriented messages exposed to end users
Check empty states: 'No entities found' or '404' is system language; 'No results — try a different search' is user language
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability principle: labels, buttons, and icons must use vocabulary and conventions familiar to users, not internal or technical terminology. This falls under the ux-usability category, real-world-language subcategory — Nielsen Heuristic 2. Users arrive with vocabulary and mental models from real-world experience. When UI language reflects system architecture or developer jargon rather than the user's vocabulary, comprehension requires extra effort. Novel icons without labels force guessing. To detect: check CTA and nav labels for internal terminology; check icons for recognisability without labels; check error messages and empty states for developer-oriented language. Applies across both viewport and full page.

knowledge.rule: Every modal, dialog, multi-step flow, and destructive confirmation must provide a clearly visible way to cancel or exit without completing the action. A flow or overlay with no exit traps users and forces them to complete it or abandon the page.
knowledge.detail: Users frequently trigger actions by mistake — tapping the wrong button, opening the wrong modal, starting a process they didn't intend to. When an easy exit is present, users feel in control and explore with confidence. When it is absent, they feel trapped. The exit must be clearly visible and labeled, not hidden in a corner, styled as a low-contrast ghost button, or absent entirely. Cancel and primary action buttons must also be visually distinct so users can confidently identify the escape path.
detection_signals: Check modals and dialogs: is there a clearly visible close button (X) and/or a Cancel button? An overlay with only a primary action and no exit is a failure
Check multi-step flows: is there a Back button or Cancel link at each step? A wizard with only a Next button traps users mid-flow
Flag forms or checkout flows where the only visible action is 'Submit' or 'Continue' with no escape path
Check that Cancel and Confirm/Submit buttons are visually distinct — if both look identical, users cannot confidently identify the safe exit
Check if the Cancel action is styled as a destructive or danger button — the exit path should never look more alarming than the action itself
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability rule: every modal, dialog, multi-step flow, and destructive confirmation must provide a clearly visible exit. This falls under the ux-usability category, user-control subcategory — Nielsen Heuristic 3. Users frequently trigger actions by mistake. When an easy exit is present, they feel in control; when absent, they feel trapped. The exit must be clearly visible and labeled — not hidden, low-contrast, or absent. Cancel and primary action buttons must be visually distinct. To detect: check modals for close buttons and cancel options; check multi-step flows for back/cancel at each step; flag flows where the only visible action is proceed with no escape; check visual distinction between Cancel and Submit. Applies across both viewport and full page.

knowledge.rule: Standard UI elements — logo, primary navigation, search, primary CTA — must appear in positions users expect based on their experience with other products. Unconventional placement forces users to relearn the interface and increases cognitive load.
knowledge.detail: Jakob's Law states that users spend most of their time on products other than yours, so their expectations are shaped by the cumulative experience of every other interface they've used. Standard placements have become conventions precisely because they work — users can find navigation, search, and identity elements without hunting. Violating these conventions is not inherently wrong, but the cost must be justified by a clear benefit. Most AI-generated UIs violate conventions accidentally, not intentionally.
detection_signals: Check logo placement: conventionally top-left (desktop) or top-center — a logo in a non-standard position is a discovery concern
Check primary navigation: conventionally a top horizontal bar (desktop) or hamburger/bottom bar (mobile) — navigation buried mid-page or only in the footer is non-standard
Check search affordance: conventionally top-right or within the navigation bar — search placed in unexpected locations requires users to hunt
Check primary CTA placement in the hero: conventionally visible above the fold, left-aligned or centered — a primary CTA below the fold with no above-fold hook is a conversion risk
This is a principle — flag only when placement is clearly unconventional and not justified by visible design intent
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability principle: standard UI elements — logo, navigation, search, primary CTA — must appear in positions users expect based on experience with other products. This falls under the ux-usability category, platform-conventions subcategory — Nielsen Heuristic 4, Jakob's Law. Users spend most of their time on other products, so their expectations are shaped by accumulated interface experience. Violating conventions forces relearning and increases cognitive load. Standard placements: logo top-left or top-center; navigation top bar (desktop) or hamburger/bottom bar (mobile); search top-right; primary CTA above the fold. To detect: check placement of logo, navigation, search, and primary CTA against standard positions. Flag only clearly unconventional placements, not deliberate creative choices. Viewport-scoped.

knowledge.rule: Irreversible or high-consequence actions must require explicit confirmation before execution. Unavailable options should be visibly disabled — not hidden — so users understand what is possible without having to discover it through failure.
knowledge.detail: The best error message is one that never needs to appear. Good error prevention removes the possibility of the error rather than recovering from it. Two distinct approaches: eliminating error-prone conditions (greying out unavailable options, constraining date pickers to valid ranges), and confirming before irreversible actions (delete confirmation dialogs, subscription cancellation warnings). Hiding unavailable options rather than disabling them creates 'mystery missing features' — users assume the product doesn't support something it actually does.
detection_signals: Check destructive actions (Delete, Remove, Cancel subscription, Clear data): do they trigger a confirmation step before execution? Single-click permanent actions with no confirmation are failures
Check date pickers and booking UIs: unavailable dates should be visibly greyed out, not absent from the calendar
Look for form inputs that accept invalid values with no inline constraint — a phone number field that accepts letters, or a date field that accepts impossible dates, increases error likelihood
Flag forms that allow submission with no visible client-side validation — sending users to a server error state when the issue could have been caught at input
Check if unavailable features or locked tiers are hidden entirely — greyed-out with explanation is preferable to invisible
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability rule: irreversible actions require explicit confirmation; unavailable options should be visibly disabled, not hidden. This falls under the ux-usability category, error-prevention subcategory — Nielsen Heuristic 5. The best error message never needs to appear. Error prevention takes two forms: eliminating error-prone conditions (constraining inputs, greying out unavailable options) and confirming before irreversible actions (delete confirmation dialogs). Hiding unavailable options instead of disabling them creates mystery missing features. To detect: check destructive actions for confirmation steps; check pickers for visible disabled states; look for unconstrained inputs that accept invalid values; flag forms with no client-side validation. Applies across both viewport and full page.

knowledge.rule: Users must be able to identify where they are in the product at any point without having to remember how they got there. Active navigation states, breadcrumbs, page titles, and contextual help reduce the memory burden placed on users.
knowledge.detail: Human short-term memory is limited. Interfaces that require users to remember information from one screen or step to use it in another place cognitive burden on every interaction. Recognition — seeing information presented in the interface — is significantly easier than recall — retrieving it from memory. Navigation context (breadcrumbs, active states, section headers) and contextual help (inline instructions, tooltip explanations) are the primary tools for maintaining orientation without burdening memory.
detection_signals: Check multi-level navigation: does the active page have a visible selected or active state? Identical appearance for all nav items gives no location signal
Look for breadcrumbs in nested flows or multi-level content — a user three levels deep with no breadcrumb trail must recall their path to navigate back
Check if the current page or section is identified by a visible title or heading that anchors the user's location

Check complex or unfamiliar form fields and settings for contextual help — a field labeled 'API Key' or 'Webhook URL' with no helper text or tooltip forces users to leave the interface to understand what is required
In long single-page applications, check if scroll position or current section is indicated through sticky navigation, active section highlighting, or a progress indicator
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability principle: users must be able to identify where they are in the product without remembering how they got there. This falls under the ux-usability category, recognition-over-recall subcategory — Nielsen Heuristic 6. Human short-term memory is limited; recognition from visible interface elements is significantly easier than recall from memory. Navigation context — breadcrumbs, active states, section headers — and contextual help — inline instructions, tooltip explanations — are the primary tools. To detect: check active nav states; look for breadcrumbs in nested flows; verify the current page is identified by a visible title; check complex fields for contextual help text; look for section location indicators in long pages. Applies across both viewport and full page.

knowledge.rule: Every visible element on a page must serve the user's primary goal on that page. Decorative content, off-topic sections, and low-priority information that occupies prominent visual space competes with essential content and reduces its relative visibility.
knowledge.detail: This heuristic is not about visual simplicity of individual components (see kb-g-004) — it is about content selection and information architecture at the page and section level. Every additional piece of content a user encounters competes for attention with the content that actually matters. A landing page with a lengthy 'our story' section above the product value proposition, or a dashboard where decorative illustrations occupy the same visual weight as key metrics, fails this principle. The question is always: does this element help the user accomplish their goal on this page?
detection_signals: Identify the page's primary goal, then check whether any prominently placed element is unrelated to that goal
Look for decorative illustrations, background patterns, or ambient animations that are as visually prominent as functional content
Check if the page has too many sections of similar visual weight — a homepage with 8+ equal-priority sections gives users no signal about where to focus
Flag hero sections where decorative imagery occupies more visual space than the value proposition or primary CTA
Distinguish from kb-g-004 (visual complexity of individual components) — this principle operates at the content and section level, asking whether content belongs on the page at all
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability principle: every visible element must serve the user's primary goal on that page. This falls under the ux-usability category, aesthetic-minimalism subcategory — Nielsen Heuristic 8. This is not about visual simplicity of individual components (see kb-g-004) — it is about content selection and information architecture at the page level. Every additional piece of content competes for attention with what actually matters. A landing page with a lengthy company story above the value proposition, or decorative illustrations as prominent as functional content, fails this principle. To detect: identify the page's primary goal and check whether prominently placed elements serve it; look for sections of equal visual weight with no hierarchy; flag decorative content competing with functional content. Applies across both viewport and full page.

knowledge.rule: Error messages must be written in plain language, name the specific problem, and offer a constructive next step or solution. Generic, technical, or vague messages leave users stuck with no path forward.
knowledge.detail: An error message that only states that something went wrong is nearly useless — users already know something went wrong because they are reading an error message. The value of an error message is in identifying what went wrong and what to do about it. 'Something went wrong' is a failure; 'Your session expired — please sign in again' is correct. Technical error codes and developer-facing messages exposed to end users compound the problem by adding language the user cannot interpret. Empty states and zero-result states follow the same rule: 'No results' is incomplete; 'No results for X — try a different search or browse popular topics' is correct.
detection_signals: Check visible error messages for specificity: generic messages like 'Something went wrong', 'Error', or 'Request failed' are failures
Flag error messages containing technical language, error codes, or stack-trace fragments visible to end users
Check if error messages suggest a next action or solution — 'Invalid email' alone is incomplete; 'Enter a valid email address (e.g. name@domain.com)' is correct
Check empty or zero-result states: 'No results found' with no suggestions, filters, or alternative paths is a recovery failure
Complements kb-w-005 which covers whether errors are identified at all — this covers the quality and actionability of the message content
knowledge.vector_embedding_text: UX usability rule: error messages must be in plain language, name the specific problem, and offer a constructive next step. This falls under the ux-usability category, error-recovery subcategory — Nielsen Heuristic 9. A generic error message is nearly useless — users already know something went wrong. The value is in identifying what and suggesting what to do. 'Something went wrong' is a failure; 'Your session expired — sign in again' is correct. Technical error codes exposed to users compound the problem. Empty and zero-result states follow the same rule: 'No results' without suggestions is a recovery failure. Complements kb-w-005 (which covers whether errors are identified) by addressing message quality and actionability. Applies across both viewport and full page.
